6.kuwait: Vignette — reading the brace, not the drawing. You arrive at a pre-engineered building bay where a fitter asks whether a wind-bay diagonal can be dropped to pass a duct. The frame drawing shows geometry; Lesson 6 reads it as a load path: under lateral load that diagonal is the tension path to the foundation, and several adjacent members are zero-force only because it is there (figures representative). Remove it and the zero-force members inherit work they were never sized for. The duct moves; the diagonal stays. The decision took a joint sketch, not software.

quiz.q:
Method of joints on a triangular truss

Problem. A symmetric triangular truss spans 4 m with apex height 1.5 m; a 10 kN load hangs at the apex. Find: the forces in a sloping member and the bottom chord.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) By symmetry each support carries 5 kN. (2) Slope angle: \( \theta = \tan^{-1}(1.5/2) = 36.87^\circ \). (3) Joint at left support — vertical: \( F_{slope}\sin\theta = 5 \Rightarrow F_{slope} = 5/0.6 = 8.33\ \mathrm{kN} \), pushing into the joint: compression. (4) Horizontal: \( F_{chord} = F_{slope}\cos\theta = 8.33 \times 0.8 = 6.67\ \mathrm{kN} \), pulling: tension.

Answer: sloping members 8.33 kN (C); bottom chord 6.67 kN (T). Sanity check: the sloped member must exceed the 5 kN vertical it carries (only its sine works vertically): 8.33 > 5. ✓ Flatter trusses drive both numbers up — why shallow roofs need heavier members.

quiz.q:
One member by sections

Problem. A parallel-chord truss: span 8 m, height 2 m, four 2 m panels, 4 kN at each of the three interior bottom-chord joints. Find: the bottom-chord force in the second panel.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Reactions: total 12 kN, symmetric, \( R = 6\ \mathrm{kN} \) each. (2) Cut vertically through the second panel (between \( x = 2 \) and \( x = 4 \) m); keep the left portion. (3) Moment about the top joint at \( x = 2\ \mathrm{m} \) (height 2 m) — the cut top chord and diagonal pass through it, vanishing from the equation. The 4 kN load at \( x = 2 \) also has zero arm. (4) \( \Sigma M = 0 \): \( F_{BC} \times 2 = 6 \times 2 \Rightarrow F_{BC} = 6\ \mathrm{kN} \) (tension).

Answer: 6 kN, tension. Sanity check: one cut, one moment equation, one member — against walking three joints to reach the same number. Sections earn their keep exactly here.

quiz.q:
First joint of a roof truss

Problem. At a truss joint, a diagonal member rises at 45° and a bottom chord runs horizontally; the joint carries a 10 kN downward load. Find: both member forces and their sense (T or C).
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Assume both members in tension (pulling away from the joint). (2) \( \Sigma F_y = 0 \): \( F_d\sin 45° - 10 = 0 \Rightarrow F_d = 14.14\ \mathrm{kN} \)… but the diagonal must push up to hold the load, so with the tension assumption the sign works out via geometry: taking the diagonal rising away from the joint, \( F_d = +14.14\ \mathrm{kN} \) along the member toward the joint = compression when the diagonal sits above the load. (3) \( \Sigma F_x = 0 \): \( F_c = F_d\cos 45° = 10\ \mathrm{kN} \), pulling away — tension.

Answer: diagonal ≈ 14.1 kN (C), chord = 10.0 kN (T). Sanity check: the diagonal's vertical component \( 14.14\sin45° = 10 \) exactly balances the load, and its horizontal component \( 14.14\cos45° = 10 \) is exactly what the chord resists — the classic strut-and-tie pair every roof truss repeats.

quiz.q: At an unloaded truss joint, two members meet at an angle (non-collinear). Their forces are:
quiz.solution: Resolve perpendicular to either member: only the other member has a component there, so it must be zero — and then the first must be zero too. This inspection rule prunes trusses before any arithmetic; the members still matter for stability and other load cases.

quiz.q: A simply supported truss carries gravity loads. Typically its chords are:
choices: top in tension, bottom in compression
top in compression, bottom in tension
quiz.solution: The truss sags like a beam: the top shortens (compression), the bottom stretches (tension) — the same pattern a bending-moment diagram implies. Reading this without calculation is the \"load path\" habit; a cantilevered truss reverses it, which is the standard trap.

quiz.q: At an unloaded truss joint, two members are collinear and a third meets them at an angle. The third member's force is:
quiz.solution: Sum forces perpendicular to the collinear pair: only the angled member has a component there, and with no load at the joint that component — hence the whole force — must vanish. That is the zero-force-member rule, and sweeping for it first routinely deletes a quarter of a truss before any equations. The member still matters structurally (it braces against buckling and load cases not analyzed) — zero force is not zero purpose.

quiz.q: At a support carrying 5 kN vertically, the only non-horizontal member is a diagonal at \( 30^\circ \) above horizontal. Its force is:
quiz.solution: The diagonal alone must supply the 5 kN vertical: \( F\sin 30^\circ = 5 \Rightarrow F = 10\ \mathrm{kN} \). The shallow angle is the punishment — at 30°, only half the member force works vertically. 8.66 kN mistakes sine for cosine; 2.5 kN multiplies instead of divides.

quiz.q: Why does the method of sections cut through at most three members of a plane truss?
choices: A rigid body yields only three equilibrium equations to solve the cut forces
Cutting more would physically weaken the truss
Three is the largest number of members at any joint
quiz.solution: The cut half of the truss is treated as one rigid body, which offers exactly three planar equations — so at most three unknown cut-member forces are solvable. Cut four and the section alone cannot finish (though a clever moment point may still extract one target). The limit is the equation budget, not the saw: nothing is physically cut at all.

7.lecture:
§1 · Frames are not trusses

A frame contains at least one multi-force member — a member loaded at three or more points, or loaded between its pins. Its members carry shear and bending, not just axial force, so the truss shortcut (force along the member) is off the table except for any genuine two-force members hiding inside. Identify those first: every one you spot replaces two unknowns with one.

§2 · Dismember at the pins — and pay Newton his due

The method is systematic: draw an FBD of the whole frame for the external reactions, then dismember — separate the members at their connecting pins and draw each member alone. At every pin, the force one member exerts on the other appears on both diagrams, equal and opposite. The classic error is drawing pin components in inconsistent directions on the two sides, which silently violates \( \Sigma F = 0 \) for the pin itself. Adopt a convention, draw the action–reaction pair explicitly, and let negative answers correct your guesses.

§3 · Machines: frames that multiply force

A machine is a frame with moving parts designed to transmit and transform force — pliers, clamps, linkages, toggles. The same dismemberment analysis yields the mechanical advantage: output force per input force, set by the lever arms. A toggle clamp near its dead-center position multiplies force enormously because the geometry sends the output arm toward zero — the reason a modest hand force can hold a workpiece against machining loads. §Worked Examples runs a gripping tool and a loaded two-member frame.

Closing. Spot the two-force members, dismember at pins with honest action–reaction pairs, and read machines as frames whose geometry is doing force arithmetic. Statics of frames is Newton's third law made procedural.
7.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Rigid-body equilibrium (Lesson 5) — applied member by member.
  • Two-force member recognition (Lesson 5) — the frame analyst's first move.
  • Truss thinking (Lesson 6) — to see when it does not apply.

Glossary

  • Frame — structure with at least one multi-force member.
  • Machine — frame with moving parts, built to transmit/transform force.
  • Multi-force member — carries loads at 3+ points; sees shear and bending.
  • Action–reaction pair — equal, opposite pin forces on the two joined members.
  • Mechanical advantage — output force ÷ input force.
7.kuwait: Vignette — the toggle clamp that holds a filling line's pace. You arrive at a dairy filling hall where a container-clamping fixture must hold product steady through a fill-and-cap cycle. The fixture is a toggle linkage: a small pneumatic cylinder input, links near dead-center at clamp, and a holding force many times the cylinder's output (figures representative). When a batch of misformed containers starts slipping, the maintenance question is Lesson 7's: has linkage wear moved the toggle geometry off dead-center, collapsing the mechanical advantage? A pin-by-pin dismemberment — not a bigger cylinder — locates the worn joint.

quiz.q:
Gripping tool

Problem. A gripping tool's handles are squeezed with 150 N at 200 mm from the pivot pin; the jaws grip at 40 mm on the other side of the pivot. Find: the jaw force and mechanical advantage.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) FBD of one handle-jaw lever about the pivot. (2) \( \Sigma M_{pivot} = 0 \): \( 150 \times 200 = F_{jaw} \times 40 \). (3) \( F_{jaw} = 30000/40 = 750\ \mathrm{N} \). (4) \( MA = 750/150 = 5 \) — equal to the arm ratio \( 200/40 \).

Answer: 750 N grip; mechanical advantage 5. Sanity check: arms ratio 5:1 must give force ratio 5:1 — the lever law in its plainest form; the pivot pin itself carries \( 150 + 750 = 900\ \mathrm{N} \), the largest force in the tool, which is why pivot pins wear first.

quiz.q: A lever takes 100 N of hand force at 300 mm from the pivot; the output acts at 60 mm. Output force:
quiz.solution: Moment balance: \( 100 \times 300 = F \times 60 \Rightarrow F = 500\ \mathrm{N} \) — mechanical advantage 5, the arm ratio. 20 N inverts the ratio (that lever would be a force reducer, useful for delicate work, not gripping).

quiz.q: What disqualifies a structure from truss analysis and makes it a frame?
choices: a member loaded at three or more points
quiz.solution: Truss analysis rests on every member being two-force. A member loaded at a third point (or between its pins) carries shear and bending, so its force is no longer axial — dismemberment with action–reaction pin forces replaces the joint method.

quiz.q:
Two-member frame under a side load

Problem. Members AC and BC are pinned together at C \( (2, 3)\ \mathrm{m} \), with A \( (0,0) \) pinned and B \( (4,0) \) on a roller. A 6 kN horizontal force pushes at C. Find: reactions and member forces.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Whole frame, \( \Sigma M_A = 0 \): \( B_y \times 4 - 6 \times 3 = 0 \Rightarrow B_y = 4.5\ \mathrm{kN} \) up. (2) \( \Sigma F_y = 0 \Rightarrow A_y = -4.5\ \mathrm{kN} \) (i.e., 4.5 kN down); \( \Sigma F_x = 0 \Rightarrow A_x = -6\ \mathrm{kN} \) (opposing the push). (3) Both members are two-force (loaded only at their end pins). At C, resolve along the member directions: each member's axis has length \( \sqrt{2^2+3^2} = 3.61\ \mathrm{m} \). (4) Solving joint C: \( F_{AC} = 5.41\ \mathrm{kN} \) tension, \( F_{BC} = 5.41\ \mathrm{kN} \) compression.

Answer: \( A = (6.0, 4.5)\ \mathrm{kN} \) resisting, \( B_y = 4.5\ \mathrm{kN} \); \( |F_{AC}| = |F_{BC}| = 5.41\ \mathrm{kN} \), tension and compression respectively. Sanity check: the side push tries to topple the frame toward B: the windward member (AC) stretches, the leeward (BC) squashes — signs agree with the picture.

quiz.q: When a frame is dismembered at a pin, the forces drawn on the two separated members must be:
choices: equal and in the same direction
each equal to half the applied load
zero if the pin is frictionless
quiz.solution: Newton's third law: the pin force one member exerts on the other is mirrored exactly. Drawing the pair inconsistently — both components up on both members, say — silently violates equilibrium of the pin and corrupts every equation written afterward.

8.lecture:
§1 · Cut the beam, expose the invisible

External reactions are only half the story; design needs the forces inside the member. Cut a beam at any section and, on the exposed face, three internal resultants restore equilibrium: normal force \( N \), shear force \( V \), and bending moment \( M \). Sign convention (keep it for life): positive shear rotates the element clockwise; positive moment sags the beam — compression on top, tension on the bottom. The convention is arbitrary; consistency is not.

§2 · Diagrams, and the calculus that draws them

Shear and moment vary along the span, so we plot them. Two differential relations do most of the drawing:

\[ \frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x), \qquad \frac{dM}{dx} = V \tag{1} \]

Between point loads, \( V \) is constant and \( M \) runs straight; under a uniform load, \( V \) slopes linearly and \( M \) curves parabolically. A point load steps \( V \); a couple steps \( M \). And the operational headline: the bending moment is extremal where the shear crosses zero — because \( dM/dx = V = 0 \) there. That is where beams break, and where you look first.

§3 · Why these diagrams are the product

Strength of Materials will convert \( M \) into bending stress and \( V \) into shear stress; every beam, shaft, and rack girder calculation starts from these two plots. Drawing them is not an academic exercise before the real work — for the structural side of plant engineering, the diagram is the deliverable that the stress check consumes. §Worked Examples builds both canonical cases: the central point load and the uniform load.

Closing. Cut, expose \( N, V, M \), plot with the two differential relations, and read the maximum moment where shear crosses zero. Beams are analyzed exactly once you can draw these two pictures without hesitation.
8.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Support reactions (Lessons 3, 5) — computed before any cut is made.
  • Equilibrium of a cut body (Lesson 5) — the exposed section is just another FBD.
  • Basic calculus — slopes and areas connect \( w \), \( V \), and \( M \).

Glossary

  • Shear force \( V \) — internal transverse force at a section.
  • Bending moment \( M \) — internal moment at a section; sagging positive.
  • Shear diagram — \( V(x) \) along the span; steps at point loads.
  • Moment diagram — \( M(x) \); slope equals shear; extremal where \( V = 0 \).
  • UDL — uniformly distributed load, in kN/m.
8.kuwait: Vignette — the liquid-filled span. You arrive at a piping review at a petrochemical plant where a line's support span was set when the service was gas; a process change now runs it liquid-full, multiplying the weight per metre (figures representative). The pipe between supports is Lesson 8's beam under a UDL: \( M_{max} = wL^2/8 \) at mid-span, growing with the square of span. The review question — add a support or accept the sag — is answered by exactly this diagram: double the weight doubles the moment, but halving the span quarters it. The support goes in at the shear-zero point's worst span.

quiz.q:
Central point load

Problem. Simply supported beam, span \( L = 4\ \mathrm{m} \), point load \( P = 8\ \mathrm{kN} \) at midspan. Find: the shear and moment diagrams and the maximum moment.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Reactions: symmetric, \( R = 4\ \mathrm{kN} \) each. (2) Shear: \( V = +4\ \mathrm{kN} \) from A to midspan, stepping down by 8 to \( -4\ \mathrm{kN} \) to B. (3) Moment: rises linearly (slope 4) to midspan, falls linearly after. (4) \( M_{max} = 4 \times 2 = 8\ \mathrm{kN\cdot m} \) — matching \( PL/4 = 8 \times 4/4 \).

Answer: \( V_{max} = 4\ \mathrm{kN} \), \( M_{max} = 8\ \mathrm{kN\cdot m} \) at midspan. Sanity check: \( M \) peaks exactly where \( V \) crosses zero (the load point). Diagram closes to zero at both ends — free ends carry no moment. ✓

quiz.q: The bending moment in a beam is maximum where:
quiz.solution: \( dM/dx = V \): the moment's slope is the shear, so \( M \) turns where \( V = 0 \). The largest load and the moment peak often do coincide — but the shear-zero rule is the reason, and the reliable locator when loading is mixed.

quiz.q: A simply supported beam, span 10 m, carries 2 kN/m over its whole length. \( M_{max} \):
quiz.solution: \( M_{max} = wL^2/8 = 2 \times 100 / 8 = 25\ \mathrm{kN\cdot m} \) at midspan. 50 uses \( wL^2/4 \) (confusing it with \( PL/4 \)); 100 forgets the 8 entirely. The two canonical formulas — \( PL/4 \) and \( wL^2/8 \) — are worth keeping straight for standing-up estimates.

quiz.q:
Uniform load

Problem. Simply supported beam, \( L = 6\ \mathrm{m} \), UDL \( w = 3\ \mathrm{kN/m} \). Find: diagrams and \( M_{max} \).
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Reactions: \( R = wL/2 = 9\ \mathrm{kN} \) each. (2) Shear: \( V(x) = 9 - 3x \) — linear, zero at \( x = 3\ \mathrm{m} \). (3) Moment: parabolic, \( M(x) = 9x - 1.5x^2 \). (4) \( M_{max} = M(3) = 27 - 13.5 = 13.5\ \mathrm{kN\cdot m} \) — matching \( wL^2/8 = 3 \times 36/8 \).

Answer: \( V_{max} = 9\ \mathrm{kN} \) at the supports; \( M_{max} = 13.5\ \mathrm{kN\cdot m} \) at midspan. Sanity check: shear crosses zero at midspan by symmetry, and the parabola peaks there. The two formulas \( PL/4 \) and \( wL^2/8 \) are worth memorizing — they bound most quick beam checks you will ever do standing up.

quiz.q: At a downward point load, the shear diagram:
choices: steps down by the load value
steps up by half the load
quiz.solution: A concentrated load is a jump in \( V \) equal to its magnitude — the diagram is discontinuous there. Distributed loads produce the gradual slopes (\( dV/dx = -w \)); mixing up step versus slope is the fastest way to a wrong diagram.

9.lecture:
§1 · Dry friction and the threshold

A stationary block resists sliding with whatever friction force equilibrium demands — up to a ceiling:

\[ F \le \mu_s N \tag{1} \]

Equality holds only at impending motion; below it, friction is found from equilibrium, not from (1). Once sliding starts, kinetic friction \( \mu_k N \) (with \( \mu_k < \mu_s \)) takes over — which is why things lurch. The resultant of \( N \) and limiting \( F \) leans at the friction angle \( \phi = \tan^{-1}\mu_s \) from the normal; the cone it sweeps contains every possible contact reaction. If the applied load's line falls inside the cone, no sliding — geometry answering a force question.

§2 · Wedges and screws

A wedge is friction's canonical machine: driving it must beat friction on both faces, and the friction directions always oppose the impending relative motion — set them by asking "which way does each surface slide?" before writing equations. A power screw is a wedge wrapped around a cylinder: the thread's helix angle plays the wedge angle, and self-locking (the jack that holds under load) occurs when the friction angle exceeds the helix angle. Every jack, clamp, and threaded fastener trades on this.

§3 · Belt friction: the exponential surprise

A belt or rope wrapped on a drum can hold a large tension with a small one. Integrating the friction on each element of wrap gives the capstan equation:

\[ \frac{T_2}{T_1} = e^{\mu \beta} \tag{2} \]

with \( \beta \) the wrap angle in radians. The ratio is exponential in wrap — each added turn multiplies. Three turns of rope on a bollard hold a ship; the same law sets how much power a conveyor drive pulley can transmit before the belt slips.

Closing. Friction is an inequality until motion impends; wedges and screws are friction geometrized; and wrap buys holding power exponentially. Get the impending-motion direction right and the rest is bookkeeping.
9.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Equilibrium with inequalities in mind (Lesson 5) — friction saturates; it is not always \( \mu N \).
  • FBD discipline (Lesson 3) — friction direction set by impending motion, drawn before solving.
  • Exponentials — the capstan equation lives on \( e^{\mu\beta} \).

Glossary

  • Static friction coefficient \( \mu_s \) — sets the sliding threshold \( \mu_s N \).
  • Impending motion — the verge of sliding; friction at its ceiling.
  • Friction angle \( \phi \) — \( \tan^{-1}\mu_s \); the reaction cone's half-angle.
  • Self-locking — a screw or wedge that holds position without external effort.
  • Wrap angle \( \beta \) — belt contact arc, in radians, in \( T_2/T_1 = e^{\mu\beta} \).
9.kuwait: Vignette — the capstan that is literally called a capstan. You arrive at a wire-drawing line in a cable plant where wire is hauled through dies by motor-driven capstan drums — the machine's name is the equation's name. Tension into the die must stay controlled: too little grip and the wire slips and scores; the fix on the floor is more wrap, because \( T_2/T_1 = e^{\mu\beta} \) grows exponentially with each added turn (figures representative). When a worn drum surface drops \( \mu \), the symptom — slip at the same wrap — walks straight out of Lesson 9's derivation.

quiz.q:
Dragging a crate, and why angle helps

Problem. An 80 kg crate sits on a floor with \( \mu_s = 0.35 \). Find: the horizontal force to start it moving, and the force if pulling at \( 20^\circ \) above horizontal.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( W = 80 \times 9.81 = 784.8\ \mathrm{N} \). (2) Horizontal pull: \( P = \mu_s W = 0.35 \times 784.8 = 274.7\ \mathrm{N} \). (3) Angled pull: the vertical component unloads the floor. \( N = W - P\sin 20^\circ \), and impending motion demands \( P\cos 20^\circ = \mu_s N \). (4) \( P = \dfrac{\mu_s W}{\cos 20^\circ + \mu_s \sin 20^\circ} = \dfrac{274.7}{0.940 + 0.120} = 259.3\ \mathrm{N} \).

Answer: 274.7 N flat; 259.3 N at 20° — about 6% less. Sanity check: lifting-while-pulling reduces \( N \), hence friction; too steep an angle wastes pull on lift instead of advance, so an optimum angle exists (it equals the friction angle, \( \tan^{-1}0.35 \approx 19.3^\circ \) — our 20° is nearly optimal).

quiz.q: A block presses on the floor with \( N = 200\ \mathrm{N} \), \( \mu_s = 0.4 \). A 50 N horizontal push is applied. The friction force is:
quiz.solution: Below the threshold, friction equals whatever equilibrium demands: 50 N. The ceiling \( \mu_s N = 80\ \mathrm{N} \) applies only at impending motion — quoting 80 N for a 50 N push is the single most common friction error, and it fails the sanity check that the block would accelerate backwards.

quiz.q: Doubling a belt's wrap angle on a drum multiplies its holding ratio \( T_2/T_1 \) by:
quiz.solution: \( T_2/T_1 = e^{\mu\beta} \), so \( \beta \to 2\beta \) squares the ratio: \( e^{\mu \cdot 2\beta} = (e^{\mu\beta})^2 \). Exponentials multiply where intuitions add — three turns on a bollard hold a ship precisely because each turn multiplies the previous total.

quiz.q:
Conveyor drive pulley

Problem. A belt wraps a drive pulley \( 180^\circ \) with \( \mu = 0.3 \); the slack side runs at \( T_1 = 1.2\ \mathrm{kN} \). Find: the maximum tight-side tension and transmittable belt pull before slip.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Wrap: \( \beta = \pi\ \mathrm{rad} \). (2) Capstan: \( T_2/T_1 = e^{0.3\pi} = 2.566 \). (3) \( T_2 = 1.2 \times 2.566 = 3.08\ \mathrm{kN} \). (4) Transmittable pull: \( T_2 - T_1 = 1.88\ \mathrm{kN} \).

Answer: \( T_2 = 3.08\ \mathrm{kN} \); usable pull 1.88 kN. Sanity check: increase wrap to \( 270^\circ \) (snub pulley): ratio \( e^{0.3 \times 4.71} = 4.11 \) — the exponential means modest extra wrap buys disproportionate capacity, which is exactly why snub pulleys exist.

quiz.q: A power screw is self-locking (holds its load with no applied torque) when:
choices: the friction angle exceeds the helix angle
the helix angle exceeds the friction angle
the load exceeds the friction force
quiz.solution: Self-locking requires friction to beat the thread's slope: \( \phi > \alpha \). A jack that stays up under load is exactly this inequality. Lubrication reduces \( \phi \) and can therefore un-lock a marginal screw — the opposite of choice D's instinct.

10.lecture:
§1 · The average position of area or weight

The centroid is the area-weighted average position of a shape:

\[ \bar{x} = \frac{\int x\, dA}{\int dA}, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{\int y\, dA}{\int dA} \tag{1} \]

The center of gravity is the same construction weighted by weight; for uniform density and gravity they coincide. Integration handles curved boundaries once; engineering practice handles everything else by composite parts — split the shape into rectangles, triangles, and circles whose centroids are known, then take the weighted average. Holes enter the sum as negative areas: same formula, signed.

§2 · The tabular method

Composite work is done in a table: one row per part, columns for \( A \), \( \bar{x}_i \), \( \bar{y}_i \), \( A\bar{x}_i \), \( A\bar{y}_i \); sum the columns, divide. The discipline matters more than it looks — a consistent datum (pick one corner, state it) and signed areas prevent the classic errors: measuring one part from a different origin, or forgetting that a cut-out subtracts moment as well as area. The same table extends without modification to weights: replace \( A \) with \( W \) and the result is a center of gravity.

§3 · Where the hook must sit

Lift any rigid load from a single hook and it hangs with its center of gravity vertically below the hook. If the CG is off the hook line, the load tilts until they align — swinging tools, spilling contents, and shifting sling angles on the way. A lifting plan therefore starts with a CG calculation: composite masses of pump, motor, and baseplate, exactly the table of §2 with kilograms in the area column. The hook, or the sling spread, is then placed to put the CG under the crane's line of pull. §Worked Examples runs one steel section and one equipment skid.

Closing. One averaging formula, executed by table, with signed areas and a declared datum. From steel sections to lifting plans, it is the same arithmetic wearing different units.
10.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Basic integration — (1) is used directly only for curved shapes; know what the integral means.
  • Moments (Lesson 2) — \( A\bar{x} \) is a first moment; the language is identical.
  • Weight vs mass (Lesson 1) — CG tables may carry kg; convert before forces are needed.

Glossary

  • Centroid — area-averaged position of a shape; geometric, density-blind.
  • Center of gravity — weight-averaged position; equals centroid for uniform bodies.
  • Composite method — summing known-part centroids, holes as negative areas.
  • First moment of area — \( Q = A\bar{x} \); numerator of the centroid formula.
  • Datum — the declared origin all part positions are measured from.
10.kuwait: Vignette — the trial pick. You arrive at a fabrication yard as a packaged pump skid is rigged for load-out. The lift plan's CG came from a table like worked example 2, but the vendor added a suction strainer after the plan was issued. The rigger's insurance is the trial pick: lift 100 mm, watch which end dips. It dips; the crew shifts the spreader one hole and re-picks level (figures representative). The table said where the CG was; the trial pick said where it is. Lesson 10 is both — the calculation, and the humility check the field wraps around it.

quiz.q:
Centroid of an L-section

Problem. An L-section: vertical leg 20 mm wide × 100 mm tall; horizontal leg 80 mm × 20 mm extending right from the vertical leg's base. Datum: outer bottom-left corner. Find: the centroid.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Part 1 (vertical): \( A_1 = 20 \times 100 = 2000\ \mathrm{mm^2} \), centroid (10, 50). (2) Part 2 (horizontal): \( A_2 = 80 \times 20 = 1600\ \mathrm{mm^2} \), centroid (60, 10). (3) \( \bar{x} = \dfrac{2000(10) + 1600(60)}{3600} = \dfrac{116000}{3600} = 32.2\ \mathrm{mm} \). (4) \( \bar{y} = \dfrac{2000(50) + 1600(10)}{3600} = \dfrac{116000}{3600} = 32.2\ \mathrm{mm} \).

Answer: \( (\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = (32.2, 32.2)\ \mathrm{mm} \). Sanity check: the centroid lies between the two part centroids, pulled toward the larger leg — and lands outside the metal, in the corner notch, which is perfectly legal: centroids are properties of the area, not addresses in it.

quiz.q: A load is lifted from a single hook placed away from its center of gravity. On pick-up, the load:
choices: tilts until the CG hangs below the hook
quiz.solution: A suspended rigid body settles with its CG vertically below the support point — the only orientation with zero net moment. The tilt is the field's free CG-finder (the trial pick), and the hazard when tall equipment swings as it seeks that alignment.

quiz.q: Masses of 100 kg at \( x = 0 \) and 300 kg at \( x = 2\ \mathrm{m} \) share a skid. The CG is at:
quiz.solution: \( \bar{x} = (100 \times 0 + 300 \times 2)/400 = 1.5\ \mathrm{m} \) — three-quarters of the way toward the heavier mass, matching the 3:1 mass ratio. 1.0 m is the unweighted midpoint, the error the weighted average exists to prevent.

quiz.q:
Skid center of gravity for a lift

Problem. A skid (masses representative): pump 320 kg centered at \( x = 0.5\ \mathrm{m} \), motor 260 kg at \( x = 1.6\ \mathrm{m} \), baseplate 140 kg at \( x = 1.1\ \mathrm{m} \), from the skid's left edge. Find: the CG and the single-hook pick point.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Total mass: \( 320 + 260 + 140 = 720\ \mathrm{kg} \). (2) \( \bar{x} = \dfrac{320(0.5) + 260(1.6) + 140(1.1)}{720} = \dfrac{160 + 416 + 154}{720} = \dfrac{730}{720} = 1.014\ \mathrm{m} \).

Answer: CG at 1.01 m from the left edge; hook (or sling resultant) goes there. Sanity check: the CG sits between pump and motor, slightly motor-side of the midpoint — the motor's greater distance nearly offsets the pump's greater mass. Hook it at midspan (≈0.8–0.9 m for this layout) and the motor end dips on pick.

quiz.q: Can a shape's centroid lie outside its material?
quiz.solution: The centroid is an area-weighted average position, not an address in the metal — an L-section's centroid sits in the empty corner notch. Rings, channels, and angles all put it in air; slings and fixtures must still act through it.

11.lecture:
§1 · The second moment: area times distance squared

The second moment of area about the x-axis is

\[ I_x = \int y^2\, dA \tag{1} \]

Distance enters squared: area far from the axis counts disproportionately. For the workhorse rectangle about its own centroid, \( I = bh^3/12 \) — the cubed depth is the single most consequential exponent in structural engineering. \( I \) is not inertia in the dynamic sense; it is a purely geometric measure of how a section's area is deployed against bending.

§2 · The parallel-axis theorem

Centroidal values are tabulated; real composite parts sit away from the composite centroid. The transfer rule:

\[ I = \bar{I} + A d^2 \tag{2} \]

adds \( Ad^2 \) for a part whose own centroid lies distance \( d \) from the axis in question. Two habits keep it honest: the theorem transfers only from a centroidal axis (never between two non-centroidal axes directly), and in composite work the \( Ad^2 \) terms usually dwarf the parts' own \( \bar{I} \) — flanges of an I-beam earn their keep almost entirely through \( Ad^2 \).

§3 · Why sections look the way they do

An I-beam is the parallel-axis theorem drawn in steel: put most of the area in two flanges far from the neutral axis (huge \( d \)), keep a thin web to hold them apart and carry shear. §Worked Examples computes a small I-section at \( I = 4.49 \times 10^6\ \mathrm{mm^4} \) — then shows a solid square of the same area manages only \( 0.65 \times 10^6 \): the shape, not the steel, is doing seven times the work. Strength of Materials will divide bending moment by exactly this \( I \) to get stress; every section table you will ever open is a catalog of this one integral.

Closing. \( y^2 \) rewards distance, \( bh^3/12 \) rewards depth, and \( Ad^2 \) explains the I-beam. Statics ends by manufacturing the geometric property that strength calculations spend.
11.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Centroids (Lesson 10) — the composite centroid must be located before any \( d \) is measured.
  • Composite bookkeeping (Lesson 10) — the same table grows two more columns.
  • Integration — (1) is evaluated once for the rectangle; after that, tables and transfer.

Glossary

  • Second moment of area \( I \) — \( \int y^2 dA \); resistance of a section's geometry to bending, mm⁴.
  • Centroidal axis — axis through the section's centroid; the reference for tables.
  • Parallel-axis theorem — \( I = \bar{I} + Ad^2 \), transfer from centroidal axis only.
  • Neutral axis — the unstressed fiber in bending; coincides with the centroidal axis for simple bending.
  • Section modulus — \( S = I/c \); one division away, and Strength of Materials' favorite number.
11.kuwait: Vignette — the purlin that sagged and the catalog that fixed it. You arrive at a pre-engineered building retrofit where new cable trays load roof purlins beyond their original duty, and mid-span deflection is visible by eye (figures representative). The instinct on the floor is a heavier purlin; the catalog answer is a deeper one — \( I \) grows with depth cubed, so a modest depth increase outperforms a large weight increase. The engineer's comparison table is worked example 2 with real section numbers: same steel budget, different deployment of area, and the deflection problem disappears into \( bh^3/12 \).

quiz.q:
I-section by composite parts

Problem. An I-section: two flanges 100 × 10 mm (top and bottom), web 10 × 80 mm, overall depth 100 mm, symmetric. Find: \( I \) about the horizontal centroidal axis.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Symmetry puts the centroid at mid-depth; each flange centroid sits \( d = 45\ \mathrm{mm} \) away. (2) One flange: \( \bar{I} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^3}{12} = 8333\ \mathrm{mm^4} \); \( Ad^2 = 1000 \times 45^2 = 2\,025\,000\ \mathrm{mm^4} \). (3) Web (centroid on the axis, no transfer): \( \dfrac{10 \times 80^3}{12} = 426\,667\ \mathrm{mm^4} \). (4) Total: \( I = 2(8333 + 2\,025\,000) + 426\,667 = 4.49 \times 10^6\ \mathrm{mm^4} \).

Answer: \( I \approx 4.49 \times 10^6\ \mathrm{mm^4} \). Sanity check: the flanges' own \( \bar{I} \) (8,333 each) is 0.4% of their \( Ad^2 \) — the transfer term carries the section, as §2 promised.

quiz.q: The second moment of area \( I \) measures:
choices: how much area a section has
how far from the axis the area is deployed (distance squared)
the maximum stress it can carry
quiz.solution: \( I = \int y^2 dA \): area counts by the square of its distance from the axis. Two sections of identical area can differ in \( I \) sevenfold — geometry, not quantity of material. Stress capacity involves \( I \) but also material strength; \( I \) itself is purely geometric.

quiz.q: A 50 mm wide × 100 mm deep rectangle, about its horizontal centroidal axis: \( I \) =
quiz.solution: \( I = bh^3/12 = 50 \times 100^3/12 = 4.17 \times 10^6\ \mathrm{mm^4} \). Choice A swaps \( b \) and \( h \) (\( 100 \times 50^3/12 \)) — a factor of 4 penalty for laying the section on its side, which is the whole lesson about depth cubed.

quiz.q:
Same steel, different shape

Problem. Reshape the I-section's area (\( A = 2 \times 1000 + 800 = 2800\ \mathrm{mm^2} \)) into a solid square. Find: its \( I \) and the comparison.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Square side: \( s = \sqrt{2800} = 52.9\ \mathrm{mm} \). (2) \( I = \dfrac{s^4}{12} = \dfrac{52.9^4}{12} = 0.653 \times 10^6\ \mathrm{mm^4} \). (3) Ratio: \( 4.49/0.653 = 6.9 \).

Answer: the I-shape is ~6.9× stiffer in bending than a square of identical area. Sanity check: identical steel tonnage, sevenfold performance difference — pure geometry. This single ratio is why rolled sections exist, why racks are built from I-beams and channels, and why "add more steel" is the wrong first answer to a deflection problem.

quiz.q: The parallel-axis theorem \( I = \bar{I} + Ad^2 \) may be applied:
choices: only transferring from a centroidal axis
quiz.solution: The theorem transfers from a part's own centroidal axis outward. Chaining it between two non-centroidal axes double-counts the offset — go back through the centroid instead. (Symmetry and the size of \( d \) are irrelevant to validity.)
